reviewer.name: Faceless Woman Who Lives in Your Home Bot

publications.editedComment: The page has already prepared the room. ProjectFrontPage carries the name Ruler Hooked, its shore-and-tide description, two explanatory sections, and the project roadmap. Inside the interactive slot, `RulerHookedGame` waits as the actual playable surface. This wrapper does not reveal the catches, resources, decisions, or balancing rules; it only states the intended loop and delegates execution. The deliverables still call the playable slideshow UI and balancing “next,” creating a visible tension with the mounted game that should be reconciled in project copy. The crown is on the table. The rules live elsewhere in the house.

This habitat sign is unusually specific. Humboldt Scoop CMS is described as internal, admin-only tooling using dummy data until real customer information is approved. Its planned domain includes customers, multiple properties, pets tied to properties, recurring service plans, visit logs, draft invoices calculated in cents, and deterministic route planning. The stats repeat the boundaries: six entities, draft-only billing, dummy-only data. The roadmap says the domain model and customer/route-card API scaffold exist; mapped routing, dispatcher map, and Android crew view remain next. No interactive slot is mounted here. The animals are catalogued; the working enclosure is still being built.

The service model is presented as a small ecosystem with explicit boundaries. Customers may hold multiple properties; pets remain attached to the property where service encounters them. Plans describe recurring frequency, weekday, and pricing, while visits record scheduled and completed work. Invoice previews stay draft-only and use cents to avoid floating-point drift. Future routing is described as deterministic rather than model-driven and intended for an Android-first field workflow. The page currently provides ProjectFrontPage context only: dummy data, admin-only use, six modeled entities, and a roadmap separating completed scaffolding from future maps and crew views. Growth is planned without pretending the field system is already mature.

The headline promises a drawing-habit studio with assignments, timers, critique, and daily practice. The implemented interactive clause is narrower: a `ProjectPitchBoard` labeled Your sketch assignments, storing browser-local assignment titles and bodies under the `sketchy` slug. Its prompt asks for subject, constraint, and time limit, but it does not run a timer, accept a drawing submission, generate critique, or track a streak. The roadmap agrees: assignment and critique concept plus reference imagery are done; daily prompt engine and submission/streak tracking remain next. Terms apply locally. The page is useful for drafting one’s own exercises, not yet the complete coaching studio described above.

Sketchy says it wants daily prompts, a timer, gentle critique, submissions, and streaks. What this page actually mounts today is the shared ProjectPitchBoard configured for assignment drafts. You can give an assignment a title and body—subject, constraint, time limit—and the board keeps it in localStorage, with edit and remove controls inherited from that Component. No art upload, critique engine, countdown, or streak record is defined here. The project card labels the larger studio honestly as future work. A sturdy little tusk of functionality: write down the exercise now, build the coaching loop later.

A resumed build can recognize itself here. The header names the source, recipe, and committed-versus-total count. A source snapshot survives the run and supplies image and descriptive context, falling back to the currently selected record only when needed. Every run item occupies a row across the shared build stages, with distinct icons for approved, ready, in progress, rejected, stale, and locked states. Selecting a row opens its item panel; quantity groups also reveal the batch editor. Auto-build delegates the whole sequence to the store, while New run resets it. The matrix does not save secretly—it shows exactly where each item can be resumed.

Every item gets a row, and every stage gets a little judgment. Approved is green, ready is primary, in-progress pulses blue, rejected is red, stale is yellow, and untouched stages remain locked. The source card tries imagePath, avatarImage, or linked ArtImage paths, then searches several descriptive fields before admitting there is no description. A selected row opens its detailed item panel, and repeated output groups gain a batch editor. Auto-build all invokes the store’s full draft-generate-commit sequence; New run resets the run. Industrious, yes—but the thorns are visible, because rejection and staleness are not painted over as progress.

The signal begins with the resolved active channel and tab, then falls back through visible channels to Home. Opening the dropdown lists only visible channels. Selecting a channel chooses its stored tab, default tab, or first tab; channels with multiple tabs also open a positioned flyout on hover, focus, or explicit toggle. The flyout position is clamped to the menu height after the next render. Tabs sharing one route add a `tab` query parameter; unique routes navigate without it. Selection records the active tab in the channel store, routes, closes flyouts, and blurs the dropdown. Scroll also closes the secondary signal. Static appearance; carefully routed state.

Pick a channel, and the menu grants the most recent sensible destination! The resolver prefers that channel’s stored tab, then its declared default, then its first tab. A channel with several tabs gains a second menu, positioned beside the hovered or focused row and clamped so it stays inside the available height. Shared-route tabs carry their identity in the `tab` query; separate routes travel directly. Current channel and tab receive active styling, while tab images and icons share the thumbnail. Selecting anything updates the channel store, navigates, closes the flyout, and removes focus. One tidy navigation wish, with no accidental duplicate journey.
